behaviors.description: Command segmentation treats newline-separated statements as separate segments, not one monolithic block.
behaviors.justification: Behavior quote: "newline-separated statements as separate segments." This targets segmentation logic at a single parser boundary. MOCK-MISS: failure is in one function/class’s splitting logic, not a cross-module handoff. REAL-INFRA/LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none required. Rejected higher level: no behavior text requiring filesystem, subprocess, network, or model decisions.
behaviors.test_description: Call the command-segmentation function with a multi-line shell snippet and assert returned segments are per-statement (assignments and command split), not one combined segment.

behaviors.description: A multi-line shell block with variable assignments followed by `gh pr create` is recognized as a PR-create trigger.
behaviors.justification: Behavior quote: "multi-line shell block ... followed by `gh pr create` is recognized as a PR-create trigger." The incident was misclassification of the whole block as `not_a_trigger`, which appears at the boundary between segmentation and trigger classification. Unit tests for classifier alone can miss this handoff bug (MOCK-MISS). INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no real OS/network/subprocess required by behavior text, (b) no AI output dependency, (c) no multi-agentic chain, (d) no LLM stage handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Wire real segmentation + trigger-detection modules in-process. Feed a block with newline variable assignments then `gh pr create`; assert final classification is PR-create trigger (not `not_a_trigger`).

behaviors.description: A similar multi-line block that ends with `gh issue create` is not incorrectly treated as a PR-create trigger.
behaviors.justification: Behavior quote: "similar multi-line block ... ends with `gh issue create`" and should not be PR-triggered. This is the same segmentation→classification boundary and guards a false positive caused by multi-line context interaction. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no required real OS/network/subprocess; no AI dependency; no multi-step agentic flow. Rejected System/Agentic/Workflow due lack of behavior text requiring them.
behaviors.test_description: Use integrated segmentation + trigger detection with a multi-line assignment block ending in `gh issue create`; assert classification is non-PR trigger.

behaviors.description: When a PR-create trigger is detected, the hook can reconstruct and persist the PR identifier needed by the review loop state.
behaviors.justification: Behavior quote: "reconstruct and persist the PR identifier." This is cross-module behavior: trigger result + identifier reconstruction + state persistence writer. A pure unit test of one function would miss contract mismatches between these components. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no explicit requirement for real subprocess/network; persistence can be validated with local in-process filesystem/temp path wiring. No AI or multi-agentic dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run hook path in-process from detected PR-trigger input through identifier extraction and state write. Assert persisted state contains expected PR identifier fields consumed by review loop.

behaviors.description: After PR-create detection, the review gate state is written in a form that the pre-tool enforcement hook can read and enforce.
behaviors.justification: Behavior quote: "written in a form that the pre-tool enforcement hook can read and enforce." This is explicitly an inter-module contract (producer hook → consumer hook), so minimum boundary is integration. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no behavior text requiring real subprocess/network/OS edge behavior, (b) no AI output dependence, (c) no multi-agentic sequence. Rejected System/Agentic/Workflow because the stated risk is schema/contract handoff, not external infra or model decisions.
behaviors.test_description: Execute post-tool state writer and then invoke pre-tool enforcement reader against that artifact in the same test. Assert enforcement logic activates correctly from the produced state.
